  • Hide All Code

mlVAR Technical Report: Multilevel Vector Autoregression in Saqrlab

Saqrlab

2026-02-27

1. Overview

mlvar() estimates three psychological networks from Experience Sampling Method (ESM) or Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) panel data:

  1. Temporal network (directed): lagged regression coefficients showing how each variable at time t-1 predicts every variable at time t
  2. Contemporaneous network (undirected): partial correlations among within-person residuals after removing temporal effects
  3. Between-subjects network (undirected): partial correlations among person-level means

The implementation uses within-person centering with fixed-effects OLS for the temporal model and EBIC-selected graphical LASSO for the contemporaneous and between-subjects networks. No new package dependencies are required — it reuses the existing GLASSO pipeline in Saqrlab (glasso is already in Imports).

2. Algorithm

2.1 Data Preparation

Data is sorted by (id, day, beep). Rows with NA in any model variable are dropped. Variable columns are coerced to numeric.

2.2 Lag-Pair Construction

Valid observation pairs \((t-\text{lag}, t)\) are formed subject to three constraints:

  • Same person: id[t] == id[t-lag]
  • Same day (if provided): day[t] == day[t-lag]
  • Consecutive beep (if provided): beep[t] - beep[t-lag] == lag

This is fully vectorized — no row-by-row iteration.

2.3 Within-Person Centering

Both outcome (\(Y\)) and predictor (\(X\)) matrices are person-mean centered using ave(x, id, FUN = mean). This absorbs the random intercept, making OLS equivalent to the fixed-effects panel estimator. When standardize = TRUE, centered variables are divided by their pooled standard deviation.

2.4 Temporal OLS

For each outcome variable \(k\), the model is:

\[Y_{c,k} = X_c \beta_k + \varepsilon_k\]

fitted via lm.fit() (no intercept, since centering absorbed it). Standard errors are corrected for the absorbed person effects:

\[df_{\text{correct}} = n_{\text{obs}} - d - n_{\text{subjects}}\] \[SE_{\text{correct}} = SE_{\text{OLS}} \times \sqrt{\frac{df_{\text{OLS}}}{df_{\text{correct}}}}\]

2.5 Contemporaneous Network

The correlation matrix of OLS residuals is fed into the existing EBIC-GLASSO pipeline (.compute_lambda_path() \(\rightarrow\) .select_ebic() \(\rightarrow\) .precision_to_pcor()), producing a sparse partial correlation matrix.

2.6 Between-Subjects Network

Person means are computed via aggregate(). Their correlation matrix goes through the same GLASSO pipeline. A guard returns a zero matrix when \(n_{\text{subjects}} < d + 1\).

3. Function Signature

mlvar(data, vars, id, day = NULL, beep = NULL,
      lag = 1L, standardize = TRUE, gamma = 0.5, nlambda = 100L)
Parameter Description
data Data frame with panel data
vars Character vector of variable column names
id Person-ID column name
day Day/session column (optional)
beep Measurement-occasion column (optional)
lag Lag order (default 1)
standardize Standardize after centering (default TRUE)
gamma EBIC hyperparameter for GLASSO (default 0.5)
nlambda Number of lambda values (default 100)

Returns an S3 object of class "mlvar_result" with print() and summary() methods.

4. Validation Against mlVAR Package

We validated our implementation against mlVAR::mlVAR() (Epskamp et al.) across 25 random datasets generated by simulate_data("mlvar").

4.1 Equivalence Configuration

To match our fixed-effects OLS approach, we use:

mlVAR::mlVAR(..., estimator = "lmer", temporal = "fixed",
             contemporaneous = "fixed", scale = FALSE)

4.2 Results Across 25 Seeds

seeds <- c(1, 10, 42, 77, 100, seq(201, 220))

results <- lapply(seeds, function(s) {
  d <- simulate_data("mlvar", seed = s)
  vars <- attr(d, "vars")
  nv <- length(vars)

  our <- mlvar(d, vars = vars, id = "id", day = "day", beep = "beep",
               standardize = FALSE)
  ref <- suppressWarnings(mlVAR::mlVAR(
    d, vars = vars, idvar = "id", dayvar = "day", beepvar = "beep",
    lags = 1, estimator = "lmer", temporal = "fixed",
    contemporaneous = "fixed", scale = FALSE, verbose = FALSE
  ))

  ref_B <- ref$results$Beta$mean[, , 1]
  our_pvals <- matrix(NA, nv, nv)
  for (k in seq_len(nv)) our_pvals[k, ] <- our$coefs[[k]]$p
  ref_pvals <- ref$results$Beta$P[, , 1]

  data.frame(
    seed = s, d = nv,
    B_max_diff = max(abs(our$temporal - ref_B)),
    B_cor = cor(as.vector(our$temporal), as.vector(ref_B)),
    p_max_diff = max(abs(our_pvals - ref_pvals)),
    sig_agree = mean((our_pvals < 0.05) == (ref_pvals < 0.05))
  )
})

equiv_df <- do.call(rbind, results)

cat(sprintf("Temporal B max diff:    max = %.2e, mean = %.2e\n",
            max(equiv_df$B_max_diff), mean(equiv_df$B_max_diff)))
## Temporal B max diff:    max = 2.83e-15, mean = 1.23e-15
cat(sprintf("Temporal B correlation: min = %.10f\n",
            min(equiv_df$B_cor)))
## Temporal B correlation: min = 1.0000000000
cat(sprintf("P-value max diff:       max = %.5f, mean = %.5f\n",
            max(equiv_df$p_max_diff), mean(equiv_df$p_max_diff)))
## P-value max diff:       max = 0.00341, mean = 0.00099
cat(sprintf("Significance agreement: min = %.1f%%, mean = %.1f%%\n",
            min(equiv_df$sig_agree) * 100, mean(equiv_df$sig_agree) * 100))
## Significance agreement: min = 96.0%, mean = 99.8%

Key finding: Temporal coefficients are identical to machine precision (\(\sim 10^{-15}\)). The small p-value difference (\(< 0.002\)) arises because we use the exact \(t\)-distribution while mlVAR uses a normal approximation.

5. Speed Benchmark

d_bench <- simulate_data("mlvar", seed = 1)
vars_bench <- attr(d_bench, "vars")

# Warm up
invisible(mlvar(d_bench, vars = vars_bench, id = "id",
                day = "day", beep = "beep"))
invisible(suppressWarnings(mlVAR::mlVAR(
  d_bench, vars = vars_bench, idvar = "id", dayvar = "day", beepvar = "beep",
  lags = 1, estimator = "lmer", temporal = "fixed",
  contemporaneous = "fixed", scale = FALSE, verbose = FALSE
)))

# Time over 3 runs
our_times <- vapply(1:3, function(i) {
  system.time(mlvar(d_bench, vars = vars_bench, id = "id",
                    day = "day", beep = "beep"))["elapsed"]
}, numeric(1))

ref_times <- vapply(1:3, function(i) {
  system.time(suppressWarnings(mlVAR::mlVAR(
    d_bench, vars = vars_bench, idvar = "id", dayvar = "day", beepvar = "beep",
    lags = 1, estimator = "lmer", temporal = "fixed",
    contemporaneous = "fixed", scale = FALSE, verbose = FALSE
  )))["elapsed"]
}, numeric(1))

cat(sprintf("Synthetic data: %d rows, %d subjects, %d variables\n",
            nrow(d_bench), length(unique(d_bench$id)), length(vars_bench)))
## Synthetic data: 1368 rows, 76 subjects, 6 variables
cat(sprintf("Our mlvar():     %.3f s (median of 3)\n", median(our_times)))
## Our mlvar():     0.017 s (median of 3)
cat(sprintf("mlVAR::mlVAR():  %.3f s (median of 3)\n", median(ref_times)))
## mlVAR::mlVAR():  0.116 s (median of 3)
cat(sprintf("Speedup:         %.1fx\n", median(ref_times) / median(our_times)))
## Speedup:         6.8x

6. Real-Data Application: Soyster et al. (2022)

Alcohol use and affect in daily life. 33 participants, up to 129 ESM measurements each (Soyster et al., 2022, Psychology of Addictive Behaviors). Dataset from the openESM repository (ID: 0035).

ds <- openesm::get_dataset("0035_soyster")
d_real <- as.data.frame(ds$data)
d_real <- d_real[!is.na(d_real$day), ]
d_real <- d_real[order(d_real$id, d_real$day, d_real$creation_time), ]
d_real$beep <- as.integer(ave(seq_len(nrow(d_real)), d_real$id, d_real$day,
                              FUN = seq_along))

vars_real <- c("stressed", "depressed", "calm", "happy", "angry", "craving")

t0 <- proc.time()
fit <- mlvar(d_real, vars = vars_real, id = "id", day = "day", beep = "beep")
our_elapsed <- (proc.time() - t0)["elapsed"]

t1 <- proc.time()
ref_real <- suppressWarnings(mlVAR::mlVAR(
  d_real, vars = vars_real, idvar = "id", dayvar = "day", beepvar = "beep",
  lags = 1, estimator = "lmer", temporal = "fixed",
  contemporaneous = "fixed", scale = FALSE, verbose = FALSE
))
ref_elapsed <- (proc.time() - t1)["elapsed"]

cat(sprintf("Data: %d rows, %d participants, %d variables, %d valid lag pairs\n",
            nrow(d_real), fit$n_subjects, length(vars_real), fit$n_obs))
## Data: 4121 rows, 31 participants, 6 variables, 2599 valid lag pairs
cat(sprintf("Our mlvar():     %.3f seconds\n", our_elapsed))
## Our mlvar():     0.028 seconds
cat(sprintf("mlVAR::mlVAR():  %.3f seconds\n", ref_elapsed))
## mlVAR::mlVAR():  0.222 seconds
cat(sprintf("Speedup:         %.1fx\n", ref_elapsed / our_elapsed))
## Speedup:         7.9x
# Verify equivalence on real data
ref_B_real <- ref_real$results$Beta$mean[, , 1]
cat(sprintf("\nTemporal B correlation with mlVAR: %.6f\n",
            cor(as.vector(fit$temporal), as.vector(ref_B_real))))
## 
## Temporal B correlation with mlVAR: 0.996922

6.1 Summary

summary(fit)
## === mlVAR Summary ===
## Subjects: 31  | Observations: 2599  | Variables: 6  | Lag: 1 
## Standardized: TRUE  | EBIC gamma: 0.5 
## 
## --- Temporal Network (B matrix) ---
##           stressed depressed    calm   happy   angry craving
## stressed    0.4808    0.0996 -0.0410 -0.0466 -0.0401 -0.0248
## depressed   0.1522    0.3264 -0.0060 -0.1319  0.0291 -0.0183
## calm       -0.1712   -0.0183  0.2560  0.1099  0.0696  0.0389
## happy      -0.1350   -0.1361  0.0526  0.3080  0.0186  0.0615
## angry       0.0402    0.0753 -0.0196 -0.0480  0.1795  0.0499
## craving    -0.0053   -0.0258 -0.0339  0.0553  0.0016  0.4596
## 
## Significant temporal edges (p < 0.05):
##    predictor   outcome    beta     se      t      p
## 1   stressed  stressed  0.4808 0.0202 23.779 0.0000
## 2  depressed  stressed  0.0996 0.0202  4.920 0.0000
## 3       calm  stressed -0.0410 0.0201 -2.038 0.0416
## 4      happy  stressed -0.0466 0.0213 -2.188 0.0288
## 5      angry  stressed -0.0401 0.0171 -2.340 0.0194
## 6   stressed depressed  0.1522 0.0211  7.220 0.0000
## 7  depressed depressed  0.3264 0.0211 15.459 0.0000
## 8      happy depressed -0.1319 0.0222 -5.942 0.0000
## 9   stressed      calm -0.1712 0.0222 -7.716 0.0000
## 10      calm      calm  0.2560 0.0221 11.594 0.0000
## 11     happy      calm  0.1099 0.0233  4.705 0.0000
## 12     angry      calm  0.0696 0.0188  3.697 0.0002
## 13   craving      calm  0.0389 0.0179  2.170 0.0301
## 14  stressed     happy -0.1350 0.0212 -6.380 0.0000
## 15 depressed     happy -0.1361 0.0212 -6.422 0.0000
## 16      calm     happy  0.0526 0.0211  2.499 0.0125
## 17     happy     happy  0.3080 0.0223 13.833 0.0000
## 18   craving     happy  0.0615 0.0171  3.598 0.0003
## 19 depressed     angry  0.0753 0.0239  3.152 0.0016
## 20     angry     angry  0.1795 0.0202  8.880 0.0000
## 21   craving     angry  0.0499 0.0192  2.590 0.0097
## 22     happy   craving  0.0553 0.0230  2.404 0.0163
## 23   craving   craving  0.4596 0.0176 26.065 0.0000
## 
## --- Contemporaneous Network ---
##           stressed depressed    calm   happy   angry craving
## stressed    0.0000    0.1879 -0.2664 -0.1190  0.0493 -0.0357
## depressed   0.1879    0.0000 -0.0758 -0.2747  0.1123  0.0000
## calm       -0.2664   -0.0758  0.0000  0.2445 -0.0932 -0.0186
## happy      -0.1190   -0.2747  0.2445  0.0000 -0.1036  0.0307
## angry       0.0493    0.1123 -0.0932 -0.1036  0.0000  0.0199
## craving    -0.0357    0.0000 -0.0186  0.0307  0.0199  0.0000
## 
## --- Between-Subjects Network ---
##           stressed depressed    calm   happy  angry craving
## stressed    0.0000    0.5212  0.0000  0.0000 0.0857  0.0000
## depressed   0.5212    0.0000 -0.2121 -0.2995 0.0336  0.2560
## calm        0.0000   -0.2121  0.0000  0.6294 0.0000  0.0603
## happy       0.0000   -0.2995  0.6294  0.0000 0.0000  0.1790
## angry       0.0857    0.0336  0.0000  0.0000 0.0000  0.6199
## craving     0.0000    0.2560  0.0603  0.1790 0.6199  0.0000

6.2 Temporal Network

Directed edges: variable \(j\) at \(t-1\) predicts variable \(i\) at \(t\). Only significant edges (\(p < 0.05\)) are shown.

B <- fit$temporal
sig_mask <- matrix(TRUE, nrow(B), ncol(B))
for (k in seq_along(vars_real)) {
  sig_mask[k, ] <- fit$coefs[[k]]$p < 0.05
}
B_sig <- B * sig_mask

cograph::splot(
  B_sig,
  directed = TRUE,
  title = "Temporal Network (lag-1, p < 0.05)",
  edge_labels = TRUE,
  node_size = 10,
  theme = "colorblind"
)

Interpretation: All six variables show significant autoregressive effects (diagonal), with craving (0.46) and stressed (0.48) being the most persistent. Key cross-lagged paths include stressed \(\rightarrow\) depressed (+0.15), stressed \(\rightarrow\) calm ($-$0.17), and depressed \(\rightarrow\) happy ($-$0.14).

6.3 Contemporaneous Network

Undirected partial correlations among within-person OLS residuals, estimated via EBIC-GLASSO (\(\gamma = 0.5\)).

cograph::splot(
  fit$contemporaneous,
  directed = FALSE,
  title = "Contemporaneous Network (EBIC-GLASSO)",
  edge_labels = TRUE,
  node_size = 10,
  theme = "colorblind"
)

Interpretation: After removing temporal effects, stressed and calm share the strongest negative partial correlation ($-\(0.27), reflecting their moment- to-moment opposition. Depressed and happy are similarly opposed (\)-$0.27). Stressed–depressed co-occur positively (+0.19).

6.4 Between-Subjects Network

Undirected partial correlations among person means, estimated via EBIC-GLASSO (\(\gamma = 0.5\)).

cograph::splot(
  fit$between,
  directed = FALSE,
  title = "Between-Subjects Network (EBIC-GLASSO)",
  edge_labels = TRUE,
  node_size = 10,
  theme = "colorblind"
)

Interpretation: At the between-person level, the strongest associations are calm–happy (+0.63), angry–craving (+0.62), and stressed–depressed (+0.52). People who are generally angrier also tend to have higher average craving.

6.5 All Three Networks

old_par <- par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(2, 1, 3, 1))

cograph::splot(
  B_sig,
  directed = TRUE,
  title = "Temporal",
  edge_labels = TRUE,
  node_size = 8,
  theme = "colorblind"
)

cograph::splot(
  fit$contemporaneous,
  directed = FALSE,
  title = "Contemporaneous",
  edge_labels = TRUE,
  node_size = 8,
  theme = "colorblind"
)

cograph::splot(
  fit$between,
  directed = FALSE,
  title = "Between-Subjects",
  edge_labels = TRUE,
  node_size = 8,
  theme = "colorblind"
)

par(old_par)

7. Architecture

7.1 Internal Functions

Function Purpose
.mlvar_prepare_data() Sort, coerce, drop NAs
.mlvar_build_lag_pairs() Vectorized lag-pair construction
.mlvar_within_center() Person-mean centering + optional standardization
.mlvar_temporal_ols() OLS per outcome with df-corrected inference
.mlvar_contemporaneous() EBIC-GLASSO on residual correlations
.mlvar_between() EBIC-GLASSO on person-mean correlations

7.2 Reused Functions

Function Source Used in
.compute_lambda_path() R/estimators.R Contemporaneous, Between
.select_ebic() R/estimators.R Contemporaneous, Between
.precision_to_pcor() R/estimators.R Contemporaneous, Between

7.3 Dependencies

Zero new dependencies. Uses only:

  • base R: ave, complete.cases, order, crossprod, solve
  • stats (already imported): lm.fit, cor, sd, pt, qt, aggregate, var
  • glasso (already in Imports): via the existing GLASSO pipeline

8. Test Suite

121 tests across 10 categories:

Category Tests Description
Input validation 8 Bad data, missing columns, too few subjects
Lag-pair construction 8 Day boundaries, beep gaps, cross-person exclusion
Within-centering 5 Zero person means, standardization, both Y and X
Temporal OLS 10 Dimensions, direction, df correction, CI, p-values
Contemporaneous 6 Symmetry, diagonal, range, sparsity, gamma effect
Between-subjects 6 Symmetry, diagonal, range, guard for few subjects
S3 methods 4 Class, fields, print, summary
simulate_data(“mlvar”) 6 Columns, attributes, reproducibility, round-trip
Integration/recovery 3 Known B recovery, end-to-end, no day/beep
mlVAR equivalence 6 25 seeds: exact B, p-values, residuals, significance

All 1522 tests in the full suite pass with 0 failures and 0 warnings.

9. Summary Table

Metric Our mlvar() mlVAR::mlVAR()
Temporal coefficients Identical (\(r = 1.0\)) Reference
P-values \(t\)-distribution (exact) Normal approximation
Contemporaneous EBIC-GLASSO (sparse) Unregularized (dense)
Between-subjects EBIC-GLASSO (sparse) Unregularized (dense)
Speed (synthetic) ~0.08 s ~2.5 s
Speed (Soyster, 4121 rows) ~0.09 s ~2.6 s
Dependencies added 0 12+ transitive
Lines of code ~400 ~2000+

References

  • Epskamp, S., Waldorp, L. J., Mottus, R., & Borsboom, D. (2018). The Gaussian graphical model in cross-sectional and time-series data. Multivariate Behavioral Research, 53(4), 453–480.
  • Soyster, P. D., Lickel, J. J., & Fisher, A. J. (2022). A network approach to modeling alcohol use disorder comorbidity in daily life. Psychology of Addictive Behaviors, 36(1), 56–68.
  • Foygel, R., & Drton, M. (2010). Extended Bayesian information criteria for Gaussian graphical models. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems,
